speaker: interviewer
text: --with Alfred Winter on July 11, 1996, in Norwalk, Connecticut. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred, you were telling us it's September in 1943, and you're in Kaiserwald concentration camp. What did you witness there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot about abuse, beatings, especially by the Polish prisoners. Of course, they hated the Jews more than the Germans did. And by the German criminals. A criminal named Mr. [? X ?] was the camp Altester, from the prisoner. And his assistant was [? Hanner. ?] His real name was Hans Brunn. And they had a contest, who could beat us, the-- the Jews faster or knock them to the ground. And, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- on a daily basis, I saw beatings of Jews by those two. Also, a lot of beatings by the Polish prisoners. So they were mostly political prisoners and hated Jews more than anyone else. And the, uh, Polish prisoners were in charge of the Jews in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a normal night like in the barracks under the Polish prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, 10:00 o'clock, usually, it was light out, but you're-- it's very hard to sleep, because traffic was going all night. And then the body lice gave you also a problem. And you were sleeping too close together, because the barracks were built, made for 250, 300, or sometimes, you had 700, 800 in a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was creating the traffic in the background, nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, people had to go to the bathroom. That was one of the main traffic. And fighting, and abuse, and-- you name it, you had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anything else there, at Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, after I had worked there for eight days, I was, uh-- a camp expansion. I was assigned to work in Spilve, on the airport. And while I was on the airport, we had to unload steel rods, which were used for making the runways in the concrete. One of the fellows got injured by unloading the steel rods, because they were tied together with a wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a Jewish fellow, with whom I had worked in a motor pool for a short time. And since there was no corpsman or anyone to help him to stop the bleeding, we tore off the sleeves from his shirt and put that on his arms to stop the bleeding. But we had to keep on working, because Polish prisoners, German capos, and the-- and even the Wehrmacht were very abusive. And worked there. When we marched back in the evening into-- into the Kaiserwald, we got stopped by [? Hanner, ?] Hans Brunn. And he said we have to work on foundation for the barracks. And we told him, we have to take him in-- in the hospital, because the bleeding did not stop entirely, severely injured. And he said, you better leave him here. I can take care better of him than they can in the hospital. We said, nothing doing. So we took him to the hospital. And I'm very sorry to say we should have listened to Hans Brunn. We took him to the hospital, and we saw him back a few hours later. And we did not recognize his face anymore. A couple of days later, he started talking with us, when we put-- were brought in-- couple of Polish prisoners were in charge there. They took off the bandages we had made for him, the shirt sleeves. And then they said it has to be stitched up. They did not give him anything for the pain, or anaesthesia, or something. Somebody put his-- stretch out his arm, and other one took his head in a hammerlock, and then they started stitching up. And every time he started moving or screaming, he would get a fist in his teeth from the Polish prisoners. Once they had finished with him, then there were two NCOs, SS NCOs, in the-- in the-- hospital. They told him that he had committed sabotage by ripping up the shirt. Of course, the shirt belongs to the Third Reich. And he got another beating. And then he got thrown out of a-- out of the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next day, we could not take him back to the-- to the airport, so he had to do work in the barracks. And it took him few days until he could eat and speak. And he had even a few teeth knocked out. Were there other incidents of mistreatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw a lot of beatings, but the-- most of the-- the worst things were going on after I had left Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe four weeks later, after I came in, I went on a volunteer basis to Dondagen. But

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported to Dondagen?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we were marched in the barrack and stripped. Then we were getting rags for clothing. All our leather shoes were taken away. We got wooden clogs with some cloth or leather on top for shoes. And then we got some other rags for a second set of underwear and a blanket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still wearing the Star of David on the outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No more, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh-- anyone who would have seen us would have known why we were prisoners, because everything was put in pieces together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the first to arrive at that camp? Was there a camp there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing. I was the first group of the 500 to arrive [INAUDIBLE] shipped by train to Dondagen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: First 2,00 in boxcars, 52 a boxcar with two guards in it. 25 on each side, and the guards with a rifle were sitting in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe three or four hours to Talsi. From Talsi, we were put on a small [INAUDIBLE] road. We were put 50 in a boxcar without guards and told to sit down. And they put-- and if-- guards on the front-- front and the back of the-- of the train with machine pistols. And said anyone who will get up during the trip will be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first sight you saw when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Dondagen, we were marched to the castle in Dondagen. And there was a big hall next to the castle with a lake. We are marched in that hall. And all 500, we sat still, had to sit down over there. Then we would get some bread and some coffee. We stayed overnight. Next morning was-- we were chased out to wash up in the lake. And then was roll call, and then we were divided in different work kommandos.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month is it now, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: October.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you hearing anything about, um, ghetto Riga? Any kind of rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, we heard the, uh, rumors [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in, beginning of November, when another transport from the Kaiserwald came to Dondagen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those rumors about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That the ghetto has been dissolved. Some have been put in [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means different work kommandos outside of the camp. The rest went to Kaiswerwald, and a big transport has been shipped to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And most of the old people and all the children are sent to Auschwitz. That what I heard. So when I ask about my parents, nobody knew anything. And everyone said, we don't know anything, because we were marched out. We don't know where we were and what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, uh-- a police officer who was in Dondagen, he was a bigger thief than I was-- had to go to Riga to the so-called SS [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food magazine, which was next to army clothing depot. And I know that my sister or my brother-in-law were working there already in the clothing depot. And I asked him if he could find out what happened to my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, since you're a child of the PX, the next time we get rations, that cost you a bottle of cognac. I told him, no-- no big deal. Instead of regular whiskey, you get a bottle of cognac.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to Riga, where they were working. And he was walking around, he saw Jews working, loading boxcars with clothing. So he-- he ask for my brother-in-law, and my brother-in-law was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he asked about my parents, so my brother-in-law gave him a slip, said my youngest brother, sister-in-law, and my father, and my sister [INAUDIBLE], they were working in the army clothing depot. My mother had been step-- it was stepmother in that case-- had been sent to Auschwitz on, uh-- on November 2nd, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you receive this information?

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me the slip.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know what Auschwitz was, but I did not feel good, either. Because the family was separated, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you continue to do at Dondagen?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, it was a potato house, the storing of the potatoes and picking up the potato sacks of farmers. While others were building the food depot on the PX. Then we had to unload all the food to put in the food depot, and-- somebody blows a whistle, because we were stealing another 25 rations [INAUDIBLE] for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you stealing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sardines in oil. [LAUGHS] But I didn't get caught. Somebody else got caught, and he blew the whistle on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyday, your job was to store the potatoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, or-- or unload the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell them you had experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that story--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they ask if somebody knows how to store potatoes in wintertime on the outside. And I told them I am a farmer. Because I knew that from Germany how that was being done, especially with the cattle feed, because the beets which you feed the cattle and everything. When they asked me how you do it, and I explained that to him, said, yeah, you know how. And that's how I got in that work kommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in that work kommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say probably until middle of December. And then I-- was put in charge of the PX.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The PX was-- let's say 25 Jews were taken out of the camp. We were deloused, got clean clothes, underwear, double set of underwear, another clothing. And two clean blankets. And march in a special building, separate building by the PX, where 20 men slept in one room, and five women in the other room. In the middle was a kitchen. 19, 18 Jews worked in the food magazine. That means 15, actually, because three worked in the PX. And four women worked in the food magazine. And one woman and another fellow worked in the housing for the SS. The woman had to do the cooking for the SS. And we had a Latvian fellow who was the shift cook in main hotel in Hamburg, [INAUDIBLE]. He was our cook in Dondagen. So we had better meals, prepared meals, than the SS had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why you were selected for this special assignment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was very strong and was very good at math, or knew how to handle certain things. Which a lot of them did not, because they had grown up in the country. So they picked out the-- the most, uh-- knowledgeable, and the strongest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job like daily in the PX?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Germans had rations, what the soldiers could get an alcohol, cigarettes, razor blade, shaving cream, and everything. And the unit would say, we have so many men, and we had to figure out rations and everything. And then we would make that ready, and then they pay for it in advance. And then they would sell it to the soldiers again. And that was my job. And it was an Unterscharfuhrer, Karl Fuks. He was from Vienna, was in charge of the PX. And he was an alcoholic. He was more drunk than sober. And a few times, I had to disarm him-- otherwise, he would have blown us over, and his brains out, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He never abused anyone. And then was in charge of the whole thing, was an Oberscharfuhrer, [INAUDIBLE]. He never abused anyone, but he was a pusher. Because the more he could push, the higher he was respected.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by a pusher?

speaker: interviewee
text: If the day had 30 hours, he would have worked 30 hours if necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the work progressed at this camp, were there rumors circulating? Or were you just involved with your work at the PX?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were a lot of things were going on. First, in there was the big camp, there came an Oberscharfuhrer, Groscher, in charge. Now, we went to the big camp on and off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the big camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dondagen. The big camp, there were over 1,000 Jews. And first, the camp Altester was [? Hans Stanburg ?] and Klaus Blumenthal. [? Stanburg ?] was from the transport Dusseldorf, Blumenthal was from Berlin, who had come to Riga in September 2nd, 1942 with the last transport from where Jews came in the ghetto. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Groscher was stealing most of the food which were in the camp. So there was complete undernourishment, and a lot of Jews were dying. And he had the bodies thrown in the Baltic Sea. And the Latvian, they're complaining that bodies were washing up on the shores when they were-- fisherman were catching the bodies in their nets. So they brought the bodies back to the camp, and I had to pick-- pick all and throw the bodies in. He had stones thrown on, and then buried. And he was very abusive. And quite a few died on-- of starvation. Then he cut off the water to the camp. The camp got filtered water before. So he had dug a well in the camp, but the water was not clean, because it was not deep enough in the ground. And a lot of diarrhea. And [INAUDIBLE], and more people died. And then the SS commanding officer from the entire training camp for the SS start complaining. He wanted to find out what was going on, that all of a sudden, the Jews were malnutrition and couldn't work anymore. And they put another SS man in charge. And then I found out what was going on. And then Blumenthal opened his mouth and said that Groscher was stealing [INAUDIBLE] and trading it in for alcohol with the Latvians-- especially sugar and things like that. So Blumenthal got taken away [INAUDIBLE] later, and then he got shot for accusing an SS man of theft. Well, anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another commandant came on, which was even worse than Groscher in one way. It was Gustav Zoger. He came order from Oranienburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oranienburg concentration camp, near Berlin. And Zoger had the name, 'The Iron Gustav.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He would-- would be beating right and left, but he-- he put a new camp elder in, Werner Zamwel. Werner Zamwel could convince him, said all Jews would be deloused. That no more lice here on the camp. And that was-- he-- he finished that up. And then they had to work to make the campgrounds more livable. But some were pushed quite a bit. He was hated for that. But in actually, everyone profited by it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your life improve after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in the camp anymore. I was separated from the camp, with the 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the PX barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And-- but we had contact with them. Everyday, a group from the camp came and had to work on the PX storage of the empty-- thing, goods, you know, empty bottles, cases, everything which the Germans recycled, that was it. And, uh, we knew what was going on in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then one day, they said, they took all the sick ones out of the camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were very much surprised. And then they brought new Jews in.

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, there were some from the ghetto of Libau, and some from the ghetto of Vilna. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they opened up another camp for another 1,000 Jews. And they start to build another camp nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were these subcamps called?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Dondagen-- 1, 2, and 3. And then the PX and the food store-- the food store had to supply the camps with food. And now comes the biggest swindle we ever meet, [INAUDIBLE], [? Gott ?] Huber. He got a list. It said, concentration camp Reich, concentration camp Ausland, concentration camp Jews, and partisans. Reich has the highest rations. Jews mediocre, and partisans less.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he destroyed the list. Partisans, he left. He had no choice. Ausland, he destroyed, and Jews, and eliminated Reich. So 'Food for Concentration Camp Prisoners.' So we-- the camp was getting more food than they were entitled to. We all knew it in-- in the food magazine, but we all kept our mouths shut. Where did these orders come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The orders came from-- from Riga, but since [? Hubert ?] was a bookkeeper for the SS in the food magazine, he got other papers. When the SS found out in July 1944, it was too late. So there were no more starvation was in the camp. And then there comes-- one day, we got the devil in person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was working in the PX, and SS [INAUDIBLE] showed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he saluted. And I said, what can I do for you, Herr Obersturmfuhrer? And he tells me, can't you salute?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, sorry, I'm a Jew. I'm a prisoner here. Where is your number? Where is your Star of David? And he slapped me in the face.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he said, well, who is in charge? I told him, I am in charge. The Untascharfuhrer is not here right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want this, I want some cognac, and I want this and this. I have-- didn't have anything from the PX for that and that [INAUDIBLE]. He showed me the papers. I said, I'm sorry, I cannot give you anything, but I will send the-- somebody over to the main magazine to get the Obersharfuhrer to give the OK. And then the Obersharfuhrer came on, and he introduced himself as Dr. Kurbach, chief medical officer for all concentration camps in Latvia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, I want this, this, and this. And the Obersharfuhrer said, give him this, this, and this. I told him, he's not entitled to a full bottle of cognac-- only so and so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those two had an argument. He said, well, well, well, this is [INAUDIBLE]. He said, give him the bottle, just to get him off my back. So I gave him everything. And then he said, I want to look those 25 Jews over you have here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then-- then we had to line up. He said, anyone sick? No, no one sick. So he walked with his stuff. Two days later, he made a raid on our sleeping quarters, and he found some French toothpaste and a few other things. Were they from the PX?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yup. But they were given by the Obersharfuhrer to us, because we got even a vaccination for typhoid and everything. Because he wanted to make sure that we were clean and healthy, that we would not spread any disease to the SS--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because we were handling the food. And the two had an argument. And he told them, look, I give those Jews all the food they need that they can work. And I make sure that they can take a bath, and I make sure they are clean and healthy. Because I don't want any diseases going to the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what month is it now, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] March 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: March 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yup. No, '44. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay at--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- wait, wait-- we're not finished. So in the meantime, more and more food came in, and we had to guard. Then 40 boxcars of flour came in, and we had to unload those. 20 men, and each boxcar had 22 tons of flour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was like [INAUDIBLE] day and night, and we couldn't manage. So they brought in 200 Ukrainians, SS men, to help us. And the Ukrainian couldn't manage. They fell down, off the stairs with the 200 pounds of-- 200 pounds of flour. So we had to work on that maybe for a week on a daily basis. In the meantime, we had to manage to steal a lot of things. And we had organized. And we agreed on what we steal goes for everyone. Nobody keeps something for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it go just to the 25 of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the 25 of us. When those who come to work, they would get some bread and some-- whatever we could spare. Then there was a fellow named Kurt Mayer from [INAUDIBLE]. He was in charge of meat and butter. And we got butter, and it says, 25 kilogram and 25.5 kilogram. So he and another fellow made a stamp, 0.5 on each case which has extra-- which was originally 25 kilogram, they make 25.5 kilogram. So he could steal butter, 50 pounds at the time, for us to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: That helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then we had a decimal scale-- I don't know if here in the United States, we use or not. You see, if you put on 1 pound on it, it is actually 10 pounds. And you put weight stones on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we drilled up some weight stones and put some steel shavings out, and closed them up again. So whenever the SS got meat, they were short-changed. So we had some meat surplus. And you kept that for your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the 25. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, although, we had a little taste for cognac.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a story about the cognac?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a few of the Latvian Jews were alcoholics before the war. And they had more taste than they could handle. So one of them managed to open bottles without damaging the cork. So we would make the bottle half-empty, filter the cognac through our kidneys, fill up the bottle again, close the bottle, and set a separate cases on the side. Whenever an SS unit moved out and they had to get cognac along or whiskey, whatever what-- they would get those cases with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your special cases?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
